summary: Early Italian Renaissance painter, teacher of Giotto. Known for the Santa Trinita Maesta, crucifixes in Assisi and Florence, and breaking away from the Byzantine 'maniera greca.' Referenced in Dante's Purgatorio.

works.name: Santa Trinita Maesta
works.description: Painting of the Virgin and Child enthroned, flanked by two quartets of angels. Throne base contains three arches with portraits of four prophets (Abraham, David, Jeremiah, Isaiah). Uses gold striations on blue robe for depth. Now in the Uffizi, displayed alongside Duccio's Rucellai Madonna and Giotto's Ognissanti Madonna.

works.name: Crucifixes / Crucifixions
works.description: Multiple crucifixes. One for Santa Croce in Florence was damaged in the 1966 Arno flood. One in the Basilica of St. Francis of Assisi has deteriorated so much it resembles a photographic negative. Created wooden crucifixes for churches at Santa Croce and Arezzo.

works.name: Diptych of Devotion / Kitchen Discovery
works.description: Only three of original eight panels survive. One panel depicting the Mocking of Christ was discovered in 2019 hanging in an elderly French woman's kitchen.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Teacher of Giotto. Broke away from Byzantine 'maniera greca.' Referenced in Dante's Purgatorio. Created mosaics for Florence Baptistery. Vasari begins Part 2 of Lives with this artist. Francis Bacon cited him as an inspiration. Participated in 1299 Jubilee as a pilgrim.

comprehensive_summary:
Cimabue (c. 1240-1302) was an early Italian painter from Florence, a pivotal figure in the transition from Byzantine to Renaissance art. His nickname means 'bull-headed.' He participated in the 1299 Jubilee as a religious pilgrim. Vasari begins Part 2 of his Lives with Cimabue, and Filippo Baldinucci's dictionary of artists also starts with him. His role in the rise of Renaissance art is largely extrapolated from a verse in Dante's Purgatorio, where Dante notes that Giotto severely overshadowed his teacher Cimabue.

Cimabue is most famous as the teacher of Giotto. According to Vasari, Cimabue discovered Giotto drawing pictures of sheep on a rock. In another Vasari anecdote, Giotto painted an extremely realistic fly onto one of Cimabue's paintings, fooling Cimabue into trying to brush it off. This story of artistic trickery is one of the most famous in art history. Linda Nochlin used the story of Cimabue discovering Giotto as a shepherd boy to illustrate her concept about inherent genius in 'Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?' Francis Bacon described a Cimabue painting as 'a worm crawling down' and said it inspired him to paint 'this image just moving, undulating down.'

He is credited, alongside Duccio, as one of the first artists to break away from the Byzantine iconlike style known as 'maniera greca,' possibly inspired by the Byzantine 'Macedonian Renaissance.' Vasari incorrectly attributed Duccio's Rucellai Madonna to Cimabue, which led Frederic Leighton to paint 'Cimabue's Celebrated Madonna' (actually depicting Duccio's work being carried through Florence's streets); Queen Victoria purchased it almost immediately.

His Santa Trinita Maesta depicts the Virgin and Child enthroned, flanked by two quartets of angels, with gold striations on a blue robe creating depth. The throne's base contains three arches displaying portraits of Abraham, David, Jeremiah, and Isaiah. It is now in the Uffizi, displayed alongside Duccio's Rucellai Madonna and Giotto's Ognissanti Madonna.

Cimabue created multiple crucifixes. One for Santa Croce in Florence, painted in distemper on wood, was badly damaged in the 1966 Arno flood and became a symbol of that disaster. Another crucifixion in the Basilica of St. Francis of Assisi has deteriorated so much it resembles a photographic negative. He created frescoes for the Basilica including the Assumption of the Virgin and St. Francis with Four Angels, and the upper church fresco cycle possibly includes contributions from a young Giotto.

His Diptych of Devotion originally had eight panels, of which only three survive. A panel depicting the Mocking of Christ was discovered in 2019 in an elderly French woman's kitchen. Other works include the Castelfiorentino Madonna (infant in pink-violet wrap caressing mother's cheek), the Virgin Enthroned with Angels (in the Louvre), and St. John in Pisa (the only work definitively proven as by his hand). He created mosaics for the Florence Baptistery with Corsi di Buono.
